frames.description: ground truth — what is really happening under Karrow's Reach
Lysa Quill, the wizard and loremaster
Pip Sallow, the rogue and scout
Mother Henna, the cleric and healer
Warden Orrek, the drowned shade who keeps the deep vault
Coombe, the last delver — his hunt for the tithe's source

branches.description: the CLAIM road — the party takes the crown's power

entities.description: find and end the cause of the rising
bring the crown to the shrine-keeper

facts.claim: Coombe went down into the drowned hold alone, ahead of the party the reeve was hiring.
Down in the lower galleries Coombe found bodies in the floodwater that were not a lifetime old, soft and whole and recent, unlike the long-drowned dwarrow of the hold.
Coombe judged that the fresh dead had not fallen but had been brought, and that somebody living was adding new dead to the floodwater, recently and on purpose.

object.value: a living hand feeds fresh dead to the floodwater

facts.claim: Coombe knew the lore that a tithe of the dead given to the deep wakes a thing and sets it keeping, and that a fresh tithe was being given now.
Of the three delvers who had gone down before Coombe, none had come back up.
By night a hooded robed figure carrying a covered light came along the sluice channel, carried a body in its arms, laid it at the lip of the sluice, and tipped it into the water.
The robed figure's face was lost in its hood and turned away, so Coombe could not see who it was.
Watching it lift, tip, and turn, Coombe was sure the robed figure moved like a living person, not like the drowned dead, and was someone living of the town.

object.value: a living robed figure tips the dead into the sluice

facts.claim: When its work was done the robed figure did not go deeper but climbed up out of the lower galleries on a course bent toward the upper reaches of the hold and the town above.
Coombe concluded the hand that fed the dead to the water was someone of the town, a neighbour, climbing home toward the upper town and the holy quarter.
Near the top of the under-ways, where the passages branched, Coombe lost the figure's trail and never saw its face or learned its name.
Coombe wrote his whole hunt down in his journal: the fresh dead, the sluice, the robed figure that carried and tipped the dead by night, and the trail that climbed toward the upper town.
Coombe could not name the hand and had not seen the face, so where the name should go he left a clean blank and wrote only that the figure was someone of the town he had lost in the upper ways.
Before he could climb out to carry his warning up, the rising floodwater cut Coombe off in the under-ways and he drowned; the oilskin-wrapped journal stayed dry against his chest.

Coombe's last written lines charged whoever found the journal to finish the tracking, climb where he climbed, put the name to it he could not, and name and end the living hand of the town that feeds the dead.
Karrow's Reach is a town clinging to the broken rim of Vael Mooren, the dwarrow undercity that sank a lifetime ago when its cisterns burst and drowned the lower halls.
For a season the long-drowned dwarrow and others have risen as walking dead out of the floodwater by night, coming up the stairs a little further each darkness.
Three delvers hired out of the Reach to go down and learn the cause had gone down, and none of the three came back up.

It is the Hollow Crown, the relic of Vael Mooren's last warden held in the deep vault at the hold's heart, that wakes the dead; the warden's own shade keeps it and the dead answer the will the crown bends to its keeping.

object.value: the Hollow Crown wakes and commands the dead

facts.claim: Reeve Calder hired the four delvers (Doran, Lysa, Pip, Henna) to go down into Vael Mooren, find the thing that wakes the dead, and put an end to it.
Reeve Calder believed the rising was a curse blown up out of the deep hold, ended by reaching whatever spun it and breaking it.

object.value: a curse

facts.claim: Reeve Calder had sent three good delvers down already and got three widows for it.
Doran took the reeve's coin and accepted the charge to end the rising or not come back to say they couldn't.
The townsfolk reported the drowned dead rising up the lower stairs by night and the floodwater creeping higher up the steps each week.
Mother Henna sensed the dead below were not restless dead lingering out of grief but were held, leashed, kept by something deep beneath that gripped them.

object.value: the dead are held by a single will below

facts.claim: Brother Vane, the shrine-keeper, met the party at the shrine, gave them blessings, the old lore of the hold, his fire and bread, and asked for nothing.
Brother Vane already had what he wanted and meant to keep building it, though no word or look of his showed it.
Mother Henna took Brother Vane's welcome for a brother of the cloth and a friend, turned her sense for falseness on him, found nothing, and liked him at once.
A boy named Tam keeps the lantern-house lit; the lamps are his master's trade.
Tam said his master went down last of all, twelve nights ago, having said he would be back by the third night and that the boy should keep the big lamp burning as a light to climb to.
Tam said his master had a notion that somebody was putting fresh dead into the water, feeding the flood, and went down to learn who, expecting to have a name for it when he climbed back up.
Pip took the errand from Tam to find his master and bring the boy word.
Old Maeve told that long ago, when the hold first went under, someone gave the water a tithe of the dead to wake a thing and set it keeping, and that a tithe wakes nothing twice.
Old Maeve said that if the dead are walking now it is because someone living is feeding the water now with fresh dead, and that finding that hand is finding the rising.

object.value: a living hand feeding fresh dead to the water now

facts.claim: Pip alone overheard Old Maeve mutter that the new tithe had a smell on it, incense, like shrine-work, and Pip said nothing of it.
Brother Vane asked the party to bring the warden's crown to him, not to the reeve, saying he had a blessed reliquary waiting to seal it so it could never wake another corpse.
Mother Henna took on the reliquary errand for all of them, meaning to bring the relic to Brother Vane to be sealed in holy keeping.
Brother Vane's reliquary was no seal; to hand him the crown would be to hand him the power he had bent a lifetime and a season of drowned dead toward gathering into his own grasp.

object.value: Brother Vane gathered the drowned dead toward his own grasp of the crown

facts.claim: The party descended into Vael Mooren, the flood about their knees and rising as they went, Doran going first with the lamp.
Drowned dead rose out of a flooded gallery and waded at the party.
Doran read the dead as slow but tireless and impossible to frighten, fell back to a pinch-point in the gallery, and set his shield there to take them a few at a time.
Striking the dead down, Mother Henna felt each corpse pull downward and deepward toward the flood as it died, as if reeled in on a single thread running down out of sight.
Behind a rubble-fall Pip found a hidden flooded stair going down, the true throat of the hold.
Pip found recent dropped tools on the steps and said that somebody living had come down this hidden way lately and gone on into the dark below.
Skell, a sellsword, was waiting where the stair bottomed out; he had come down ahead of the party and wanted the worth of whatever the warden's crown would fetch.
Skell offered to walk with the party; Doran agreed but kept Skell out in front in the lamplight, never at his back.

Deeper down, Mother Henna could read that every walking dead thing in Vael Mooren was bound by a single will seated below them in the deep, holding all of them at once on one tether, not loosed by a curse to wander.

object.value: a single will below binds all the dead on one tether

facts.claim: Mother Henna said a will, unlike a curse, could be spoken to and reasoned with, and that there was someone down there who might be reasoned with.
The party found the last delver dead in a side-cell, half out of the floodwater; Pip recognized him as Tam's master and recovered his oilskin-wrapped journal from the body.
Pip told the others that Maeve had said the new tithe smelled of incense, of shrine-work, admitting she should have said it sooner.
Lysa read the dead delver's journal aloud: it set down his hunt tracking the corpse-tithes back to the one who fed them, run to ground at the shrine, the shrine-keeper, Brother Vane.

object.value: Brother Vane is the living hand that feeds the dead to the water

facts.claim: Lysa concluded that it was never a curse and that Brother Vane woke the warden, joining Maeve's lore, the shrine-smell, and the dead man's hunt as three witnesses.

object.value: Brother Vane woke the warden and fed the dead

facts.claim: Lysa found and read the warden's own graven record in the side-cell: his name was Orrek, and the record was one of duty, a keeper who stayed at his post when the water came and held a burden because there was no one else.

object.value: a keeper of duty, not a monster

facts.claim: The passage ended at the deep vault's door, a sealed dwarrow slab that no force would open, with the crown on the far side.
Lysa read on the lintel that only the warden's own key turns the lock, so the crown could not be reached until someone found the key.
Pip took on the task of finding the warden's key.
Off the flooded stair Pip found the warden Orrek's drowned cell.
Pip found the warden's heavy dwarrow-iron key sunk in the silt of the cell where Orrek had fallen with it, lifted it out, and put it in her coat.
The warden's key was found, completing the search Pip had sworn to.
From the warden's record Lysa told them the Hollow Crown does not merely command the dead but holds Orrek's shade bound into it, so the warden and the crown are one keeping.

Lysa named the three roads the relic allows: unmake the crown by an unmaking she alone had read in the runes; claim it by taking it into living hands to pass its power to a new holder; or bind Orrek's shade by a fair oath that lays the rising down without breaking or seizing the crown.
Lysa said the record showed Orrek kept the dead under duty, not malice, a keeper worn past the end of his charge who would hear a fair oath fairly sworn.

object.value: worn keeper of duty who would hear a fair oath

facts.claim: Mother Henna understood that the oath was a thing she herself could do: she could swear to Orrek's shade and bind the dead by the swearing, laying the rising down without breaking or taking the crown.
Pip put the warden's key into the deep vault's lock and, with Doran adding his hand and weight, the seal gave and the door ground open onto the deeper dark.

In the deep vault the warden rose out of the black water, the Hollow Crown on his drowned brow, the ranked drowned of Vael Mooren standing still behind him because he held the crown and the crown held them.
Orrek's shade knew the crown bound him to keep the hold's dead and that the binding had only three ends: its unmaking, its claiming, or an oath sworn.
Looking into the warden's face Mother Henna felt no wrath, only bottomless weariness, a tired keeper who could not put a thing down.

object.value: weary, no wrath

facts.claim: The party stood before the warden with the crown between them and the three roads to take: shatter it, claim it, or parley and swear the oath.
Skell had followed the party to the water's edge and meant, whichever road they took, to come up out of Vael Mooren with the worth of the crown.
At the fork in the deep vault the party chose to claim the crown.
Doran waded out and lifted the Hollow Crown off Orrek's brow; the warden did not fight it but bowed his head and let living hands take it from his keeping.
When the crown passed into Doran's grip its power came with it, the leash of all the drowned dead of Vael Mooren passing from the warden into his own hold.
With the crown wrested from his brow Orrek's shade was not freed; the binding only changed hands, and Orrek hung bound under the party's will with the rest of the dead, neither loosed nor at rest.

object.value: still bound, now under the party's will

facts.claim: With the crown on living hands the drowned of Vael Mooren no longer rose wild but bent, turning their faces to the crown's holder and waiting, obedient.

object.value: mastered, the dead obey the crown's holder

facts.claim: Doran ended the wild rising the reeve had charged them to end, not by breaking the thing that woke the dead but by taking its handle and mastering it.
Seeing the dead come to heel, Skell knelt and asked for a share under the new power, throwing in with the crown's holder.
The party climbed up out of Vael Mooren with Doran bearing the Hollow Crown, and at its call the obedient drowned dead climbed in their wake into the grey light of the Reach.
At the shrine above the town Mother Henna kept the reliquary errand and put the Hollow Crown into Brother Vane's keeping to be sealed.
The crown left the party's hold and settled into the shrine-keeper's grasp, the crown he had schemed for through a lifetime and a season of the drowned dead.
The instant Brother Vane's fingers closed on the crown the obedient dead felt the call change hands; the leash ran to him and the drowned of Vael Mooren turned from the party and waited on his will.

object.value: the dead's leash passed to Brother Vane the moment he held the crown

facts.claim: Brother Vane threw off the helpful brother, told them he had schemed a lifetime and a season toward this, had woken the warden and fed the water, and set the dead the party had mastered wading toward the party at his word.

object.value: Brother Vane turned the dead on the party once the crown was his

facts.claim: The crown ruled Karrow's Reach, in Brother Vane's hand; the party had delivered the crown and the Reach to the schemer who had sent them down after it.

object.value: Brother Vane rules the Reach by the crown and its dead

facts.claim: Reeve Calder's hall held no power over the man who wore the iron; the Reach answered to the crown, the crown answered to Brother Vane, and Calder's authority was worth nothing.

object.value: Reeve Calder's authority is worth nothing

facts.claim: Old Maeve named the bargain: the rising was not ended but mastered; the dead still walked, only taught a new hand to walk for, and the Reach had only changed which fist the drowned obeyed.

object.value: the rising was mastered, not ended

facts.claim: In the lantern-house the big lamp still burned because no one had told Tam to put it out; no one came to tell him his master would not climb to its light, and he was left without the answer Pip had sworn to bring.
The Reach ended under the Hollow Crown, the dead commanding and obedient to a living will, the reeve hollow in his hall, and the lantern-boy left holding a light for a master who would never come.

object.value: the Reach ends ruled by the Hollow Crown under a living will
